explanation: The sentence requires that the controller implements (i.e., is able to enter) each of the named modes. In LTL, we express 'the system shall implement' a mode as: that mode will occur at least once (i.e., 'finally' or F), for each mode. Therefore, for each mode, we use F(mode), where 'mode' stands for an atomic proposition indicating the mode is active at some point. We combine all of them with the AND (&) operator, as the requirement is for all modes to be implemented at least once in possible behaviors.

Self-Test Mode shall be implemented
VentilationOff Mode (same as StandbyMode) shall be implemented
Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode shall be implemented
Pressure Support Ventilation Mode shall be implemented
Fail-safe mode shall be implemented

explanation: The natural language requires that the controller be able to implement six distinct modes. In temporal logic, 'shall implement' typically translates to 'it is always possible to reach and/or enter each of those modes.' For a strict implementation requirement, we should state that, infinitely often (GF), the system will reach each mode. This means: whichever path is taken, each mode will eventually be entered, and this is guaranteed to happen infinitely often throughout operation. We therefore encode for each mode: 'GF(mode)' for each atomic proposition corresponding to a mode. The full property is a conjunction: GF(StartUpMode) & GF(SelfTestMode) & GF(StandbyMode) & GF(PCVMode) & GF(PSVMode) & GF(failSafeMode).

explanation: The sentence specifies that the controller shall implement six modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and fail-safe. These modes are mutually exclusive, i.e., at any moment, the controller must be in one (and only one) of these modes. To formalize this, we express (1) that the controller is always in at least one of these modes (disjunction, globally), and (2) that at every moment, the controller is never in two modes at once (pairwise mutual exclusion, globally).

It means that, during the operation of the controller, it must be possible to enter (i.e., eventually reach) each of these modes. In LTL, this can be expressed by asserting that, for each mode, it will eventually (F) become active at least once during system execution. If there is an atomic proposition associated with each mode (as provided), then we write F(mode) for each.

explanation: The sentence requires that the controller implements (i.e., is always able to enter) a set of modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff, Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and fail-safe. In LTL, this means that it must always be possible to reach (eventually enter) each of these modes. The LTL property for 'infinitely often' would be GF(p), meaning mode p can always be entered again in the future. However, if we only require the controller can (at least once) enter each mode, we translate as F(p) for each mode, and we require all of them: F(StartUpMode) & F(SelfTestMode) & F(StandbyMode) & F(PCVMode) & F(PSVMode) & F(failSafeMode). If we require that it is always possible to reach any mode (i.e., even after any point, you can reach the mode again), we use GF(p). But the original requirement only asks for implementation/possibility, so a single F(p) for each mode suffices unless the context demands otherwise.

In LTL, we interpret this as stating that each of these modes must be reachable (i.e., possible to eventually occur) at least once during operation. We use the atomic propositions that indicate when the controller is in each corresponding mode: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (used here as 'VentilationOff'), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. To encode the requirement that all these modes shall be implemented (i.e., must be possible), we say that, for each mode, eventually at some point, the controller enters that mode. That is, for each mode proposition m, F m (Finally/Eventually m) must be satisfied. To say that *all* of these modes must occur at least once, we use conjunction: F(StartUpMode) & F(SelfTestMode) & F(StandbyMode) & F(PCVMode) & F(PSVMode) & F(failSafeMode).

explanation: The natural language statement requires that the controller can implement the following modes at appropriate times: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and fail-safe. In LTL, we typically formalize such requirements as: "Each mode is reachable infinitely often (the system can always eventually enter each required mode and never gets stuck out of all legal modes)." For implementation readiness, we can also require that 'eventually' (F) each mode can be reached. This is best expressed as the conjunction of 'infinitely often' (GF) formulas for each mode. E.g., for StartUpMode: 'GF StartUpMode', and so on. Thus, the overall LTL property is: GF(StartUpMode) & GF(SelfTestMode) & GF(StandbyMode) & GF(PCVMode) & GF(PSVMode) & GF(failSafeMode), meaning that each of the six modes will be entered infinitely often in any infinite execution (i.e., they are always available to be entered, never locked out).

All modes are always reachable repeatedly

explanation: The sentence 'The controller shall implement the following modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff, Pressure Controlled Venilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, fail-safe.' specifies that the system must always eventually be capable of transitioning into any of the listed modes, i.e., each mode must be reachable in every future. In LTL, this can be specified as: 'For each mode, it must happen infinitely often that the system is in that mode (GF mode)'. Alternatively, a weaker reading is that it must be possible to eventually reach any mode from any state (GF(mode)). For safety, we use the 'infinitely often' reading for operational modes except failSafeMode, which should be reachable if a fault occurs; but based on the prompt, we encode that all listed modes must always eventually be possible. Thus, we combine all the required modes as a disjunction and require that, globally, it is always possible to eventually reach any one of them: G(F(StartUpMode) & F(SelfTestMode) & F(StandbyMode) & F(PCVMode) & F(PSVMode) & F(failSafeMode)).
